title: Using PTFE tape and jointing compound
subtitle: Seal BSP threaded fittings against water and gas
excerpt: How to correctly apply PTFE tape and jointing compound to male-threaded pipe fittings: the number of wraps, the correct winding direction, and when to use compound instead of or in addition to tape.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. WRAS Water Regulations Advisory Scheme guidance (publicly available). Fernox technical guidance on jointing compounds.

content.text: Most threaded plumbing fittings in domestic work use
BSP threads
PTFE tape
seals these threads against water leakage. Jointing compound does the same job with a paste applied to the threads. The choice between them (or both together) depends on the application.
When to use PTFE tape

PTFE tape is used on standard domestic water and gas thread fittings: tap tails, ball valves, stopcock tails, radiator valves, boiler connections. It is suitable for both water and gas (check the tape is rated for gas where gas work is in scope: specialist gas-grade PTFE tape is available).
How to apply PTFE tape
Step 1, clean the threads.
Remove any old PTFE or compound from the threads with a wire brush or cloth. The threads must be clean and dry before taping.
Step 2, wrap the tape clockwise.

Hold the fitting with the thread pointing away from you. Start the tape at the first thread, just back from the end. Wrap the tape clockwise as viewed from the open thread end (the same direction as tightening the fitting into the female). This is critical: winding anticlockwise means the fitting unscrews the tape as it is tightened, bunching it rather than compressing it into the thread.
Step 3, tension the tape as you wrap.
Pull the tape slightly as you wrap, keeping it under light tension so it lies flat on the thread. Each wrap should overlap the previous by about a third of the tape width.
Step 4, apply the right number of wraps.

Five wraps for a standard 12 mm wide PTFE tape on 1/2-inch or 3/4-inch BSP is the standard domestic application. Do not apply fewer than three wraps (insufficient seal) or more than eight (the tape bunches in the fitting and prevents it from running up fully).
Step 5, tighten the fitting by hand, then spanner.

Thread the fitting in by hand until it is finger-tight and running smoothly. Apply the spanner for a further one to two turns to compress the tape into the threads. For compression fittings, hand-tight plus three-quarter of a turn is the standard.
When to use jointing compound

Jointing compound (also called thread sealant or pipe dope) is a thick paste applied to the threads in addition to PTFE tape. It fills larger imperfections in older or worn threads where tape alone may not seal. It is particularly useful on older imperial BSP fittings in period houses, where worn threads may have slight damage. Apply a thin, even coat of compound over the applied PTFE tape before tightening.
What it looks like when it is right

A correctly taped and tightened fitting should run up smoothly with no binding, and when the water supply is restored, show no drip or seepage at the joint. Check by wiping dry and holding a piece of tissue at the joint.
Common mistakes
Winding the tape anticlockwise.
The most common error. The tape unwinds as the fitting is tightened, providing no seal.
Too many wraps.
The tape packs out and prevents the fitting from running fully home. Do not exceed eight wraps on standard domestic sizes.
Applying tape to the female thread.
PTFE tape goes on the male thread only. It has no sealing effect on a female thread.
